title: Real Student Stories That Stick
learningObjectives: Describe at least three ways inaccessible materials create barriers for students
Distinguish between visible and non-visible disabilities in the classroom context
Explain why 'just ask for accommodations' is not a sufficient institutional response

keyTerms.definition: Software that converts on-screen text and interface elements into synthesized speech or braille output, enabling people who are blind or have low vision to use computers. Common screen readers include JAWS, NVDA (Windows), and VoiceOver (Mac/iOS). Screen readers rely on proper document structure — headings, alt text, reading order — to make sense of content.

Any device, software, or equipment that helps a person with a disability perform tasks they would otherwise find difficult or impossible. Examples include screen readers, screen magnifiers, voice recognition software, switch devices, refreshable braille displays, and alternative keyboards.

A disability that is not immediately apparent to others. Examples include learning disabilities (dyslexia, ADHD), chronic pain, mental health conditions, autoimmune disorders, traumatic brain injury, and many forms of hearing loss. The majority of disabilities are non-visible, which means you cannot tell whether a student has a disability by looking at them.

A framework developed by CAST that guides the design of learning experiences to be accessible and effective for all learners from the start, rather than retrofitting accommodations for individual students after the fact. UDL is organized around three principles: multiple means of engagement, representation, and action & expression.

Accommodations are reactive, individual adjustments made for a specific student after they disclose a disability and provide documentation (e.g., extended time on an exam). Accessibility is proactive, universal design that makes materials usable by everyone from the start (e.g., a properly structured document that works with any screen reader). Both matter, but accessibility reduces the need for individual accommodations.

In the accessibility context, any feature of a document, website, tool, or environment that prevents or significantly hinders a person with a disability from accessing content or completing a task. Barriers can be visual (no alt text), auditory (no captions), motor (requires a mouse), or cognitive (overly complex navigation).

contentBlocks.title: Who Are We Talking About?
contentBlocks.body:
When faculty hear "students with disabilities," many picture a student in a wheelchair or a student with a white cane. Those students exist, and their needs matter. But they represent a fraction of the students affected by inaccessible course materials.

According to the [National Center for Education Statistics](https://nces.ed.gov/fastfacts/display.asp?id=60), approximately **21 percent of undergraduates** reported having a disability in the 2019–20 academic year. That is roughly one in five students in your classroom. In a lecture hall of 100, that is 20 students.

The range of disabilities that intersect with digital course materials is far wider than most faculty realize:

- **Vision** — Blindness, low vision, color blindness. These students may use screen readers, screen magnifiers, or high-contrast settings. If your PDF is a scanned image with no text layer, a screen reader sees nothing.
- **Hearing** — Deafness, hard of hearing. These students rely on captions for video content and may miss information conveyed only through audio. Auto-generated captions that garble technical vocabulary are not a solution.
- **Motor/Physical** — Conditions affecting hand use, fine motor control, or the ability to use a mouse. These students may navigate entirely by keyboard, voice commands, or switch devices. A drag-and-drop quiz is a brick wall.
- **Cognitive/Neurological** — ADHD, dyslexia, autism spectrum, traumatic brain injury, anxiety disorders. These students benefit from clear structure, consistent navigation, plain language, and the ability to control pacing. A course page with no headings and walls of unbroken text is genuinely disorienting.
- **Temporary and Situational** — A broken wrist, a concussion, post-surgical recovery, a migraine. These are not permanent disabilities, but they create the same barriers. A student with a broken dominant hand cannot use a mouse for six weeks.

The critical insight is this: **the majority of disabilities are non-visible**. You cannot tell by looking at your class roster which students have disabilities. Many students with disabilities never disclose to Disability Services at all — [research consistently shows](https://nationaldisabilitycenter.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/Student-Access-Report-2025-Accessible.pdf) that a significant portion of students with disabilities in higher education do not register for accommodations, for reasons ranging from stigma to not knowing the process exists to not identifying their condition as a "disability." Building accessible materials from the start reaches these students too.

contentBlocks.title: Four Students, Four Barriers
contentBlocks.body:
These are composite stories drawn from real patterns reported by disability services offices and accessibility professionals. The names are fictional, but the barriers are not.

**Maria — The Scanned Syllabus.** Maria is blind and uses JAWS, a screen reader, to access her course materials. On the first day of her Introduction to Psychology course, the professor uploads the syllabus to Canvas as a PDF. Maria opens it, and JAWS says nothing — just silence. The syllabus is a scanned image of a printed document. There is no text layer for the screen reader to find. Maria emails the professor, who does not respond for three days. By then, Maria has missed the first assignment deadline listed on a syllabus she could not read.

**David — The Auto-Caption Disaster.** David is deaf and relies on captions to follow lecture videos. His Organic Chemistry professor records lectures and posts them to Canvas with YouTube's auto-generated captions enabled. In the first lecture, "enantiomers" becomes "any on timers," "chirality" becomes "charity," and "nucleophilic substitution" becomes "nuclear fill a substitution." David spends twice as long on every lecture, cross-referencing the textbook to decode what the captions actually mean. He falls behind. His professor never checks the caption quality.

**Aisha — The Headingless Course Page.** Aisha has ADHD and uses her screen reader's heading navigation to jump to relevant sections of long documents — it helps her focus and find what she needs without getting lost in walls of text. Her History of Modern Art course has a single Canvas page with the entire semester's schedule, readings, and policies in one long block of text with no headings, no clear sections, and formatting done entirely with bold text and font size changes. Her screen reader sees one continuous block. Aisha cannot scan for the section she needs. She gives up and asks a classmate to find the assignment details for her.

**James — The Drag-and-Drop Quiz.** James has cerebral palsy and uses a keyboard with a head pointer — he cannot use a standard mouse. His Biology 101 professor builds a matching quiz in Canvas using the drag-and-drop question type. James can see the answers he wants to select, but the interaction requires clicking, holding, and dragging items to their targets. His assistive technology cannot perform this action. The quiz has no keyboard alternative. James contacts the professor, who tells him to "just try using a mouse." He files a complaint with Disability Services.

None of these scenarios required a dramatic failure. Each one was caused by a single, common design choice — a scanned PDF, unchecked auto-captions, missing headings, a drag-and-drop quiz — made by a well-intentioned faculty member who simply did not know the impact.

contentBlocks.title: Accommodations vs. Accessibility — and the Curb Cut Effect
contentBlocks.body:
Higher education has traditionally addressed disability through **accommodations** — reactive, individual adjustments triggered by a student's disclosure and documentation. A student registers with Disability Services, provides medical documentation, receives an accommodation letter, and the faculty member implements the listed accommodations for that specific student.

This system is necessary, and it is not going away. But it has fundamental limitations:

- **It requires disclosure.** Students must identify as having a disability, navigate the documentation process, and ask for help. Many do not — especially students with non-visible disabilities, first-generation students unfamiliar with the system, and students who fear stigma.
- **It is reactive.** The barrier exists first; the accommodation removes it second. The student has already been excluded before the fix arrives.
- **It is expensive and slow.** Converting a 30-page scanned PDF into an accessible format for one student takes staff time, costs money, and may not be ready before the assignment is due. Multiply that across every inaccessible document in every course, and the system breaks down.
- **It puts the burden on the student.** The student must do the work of requesting access to materials that should have been accessible in the first place.

**Accessibility** flips this model. Instead of retrofitting access for individual students, you design materials that work for everyone from the start. A properly structured Word document with heading styles, alt text, and descriptive links works for a screen reader user, a student with ADHD, and a student without any disability — all without anyone filing paperwork.

This is sometimes called the **curb cut effect**, named after the sidewalk ramps originally mandated by disability law. Curb cuts were designed for wheelchair users, but they turned out to benefit everyone — parents with strollers, travelers with rolling suitcases, delivery workers with hand trucks, runners, cyclists. The accommodation designed for a few became an improvement for all.

The same thing happens with accessible course materials. Proper heading structure helps screen reader users *and* helps every student scan a long document. Captions help deaf students *and* help students watching a lecture in a noisy dorm room, students whose first language is not English, and students who learn better by reading along. Descriptive link text helps screen reader users *and* helps everyone understand where a link goes before clicking it.

[Universal Design for Learning (UDL)](https://udlguidelines.cast.org/), a framework developed by CAST, formalizes this principle. UDL encourages faculty to provide **multiple means of engagement, representation, and action and expression** — not as a disability accommodation, but as good pedagogy. Accessibility and UDL are complementary: accessibility sets the floor (legal compliance), and UDL raises the ceiling (effective learning for all).

The goal of this course is not to replace your institution's accommodation process. It is to give you the skills to build materials that need fewer accommodations in the first place — materials that work for the 21 percent of your students who report a disability, the unknown number who do not, and the remaining students who benefit from well-structured, clearly presented content.

About 21 percent of undergraduates report a disability, but the majority of disabilities are non-visible — you cannot tell which students are affected by looking at your roster. Inaccessible materials create real barriers: scanned PDFs that screen readers cannot read, auto-captions that garble technical terms, missing headings that make long documents impossible to navigate, and interactive elements that require a mouse. While individual accommodations remain necessary, proactive accessibility — building materials right from the start — is more efficient, reaches more students, and benefits everyone through the curb cut effect.

freeTextPrompt.prompt: Think about your own courses. Have you ever had a student who struggled with your materials in a way that might have been an accessibility issue — even if it wasn't framed that way at the time? Or think about a time you personally encountered a barrier using a digital tool or document. Describe the situation. Knowing what you know now about the types of barriers described in this section, what might have helped?
freeTextPrompt.rubric: A strong response describes a specific, concrete situation — not a hypothetical. It connects the experience to one or more types of accessibility barriers discussed in this section (visual, auditory, motor, cognitive, temporary, or situational). It reflects on what could have been different — a specific change to the material or process — rather than simply saying 'I should have been more aware.' Responses that draw on personal experience as a user (not just as a faculty member) are equally valid.
